top_categories.workout_context: At mile 60 of 100, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
85ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
8,500ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 100 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Tour de Big Bear's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before August.
At Tour de Big Bear, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Tour de Big Bear's paved mountain roads through San Bernardino forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 100 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Tour de Big Bear. Train it until it feels boring.
8,500ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
100 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 8,500ft of climbing on paved mountain roads through San Bernardino does to your power file on race day.
Low-cadence force work for 8,500ft of climbing. The slow, grinding ascents where you can't spin—you push.

race_overlay.heat: August in Big Bear Lake, California, USA can bring heat. Complete 3–4 heat exposure sessions in the final 2 weeks before Tour de Big Bear. Increase sodium intake 48 hours before race day.
race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Tour de Big Bear’s 100 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.
race_overlay.altitude: Tour de Big Bear with 8,500ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%. 7,000+ foot altitude throughout: reduced oxygen for sea-level riders; Temperature swing from 55F at start to 90F+ at lower elevation descents.
race_overlay.terrain: Mixed terrain at Tour de Big Bear requires surface adaptability. Include weekly rides on paved mountain roads through san bernardino national forest to build confidence.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on Durability, TT Threshold, and VO2max to prepare you for 100 miles of paved mountain roads through san bernardino national forest in Big Bear Lake, California, USA.
